agent_raw: The sepsis model outcome analysis cannot support the conclusion that the alert is improving care. Three confounds are present. First, the comparison crosses seasons: November-December versus July-September emergency department populations differ in illness type, acuity, and mortality rates. Winter brings respiratory illness, influenza, and pneumonia — conditions that overlap with sepsis presentations and have different baseline mortality than the summer case mix. This seasonal difference alone could explain variation in observed mortality without any model contribution. Second, two major concurrent changes occurred at model launch: a winter surge unit opened and empiric antibiotic guidance was updated. Antibiotic timing and selection are among the most impactful determinants of sepsis survival — an evidence-based protocol change has a plausible direct mortality effect that dwarfs what a prioritization alert might achieve. Third, the analysis cohort is defined by early lactate ordering, which is a clinician-initiated behavior that may have changed after the model went live or after the protocol update changed clinical workflows. If more lactate tests were ordered under the new practice patterns, the cohort includes different patients than the pre-model comparison period. Extending the model hospital-wide based on this analysis would be premature. A controlled evaluation — using either a concurrent comparison unit with the new antibiotic protocol but without the model, or a prior-year same-season comparison — is required before expansion.
all_issues_raised: Seasonal case-mix confound: winter vs. summer ED populations
Concurrent antibiotic protocol update and surge unit as competing mortality interventions
Lactate ordering criterion sensitive to clinician behavior changes
